result:
I keep returning to the idea that every human life is a long sentence written in a language no one else will ever fully understand. The grammar is irregular, the vocabulary keeps coining itself, and the punctuation is mostly guessed at. We speak this sentence aloud to the air, to lovers, to the steering wheel at 2 a.m., and we die mid-clause, often without even an ellipsis to suggest continuity. Yet we are convinced that someone, somewhere, will be able to diagram the whole thing posthumously, as if our existence were a kind of holy Mad Lib that just needs the right blanks filled in by an omniscient editor.

The first time this struck me with the force of revelation rather than metaphor, I was standing in the cereal aisle of a 24-hour grocery store, fluorescent lights humming like cheap angels overhead. I had come in for nothing more than milk, but I got distracted by the boxes of cereal, those colorful little billboards promising that if I ate their contents I would be “extreme,” “magically delicious,” or at least in possession of “100% daily value of 12 vitamins and minerals.” I realized that every box was a compressed novella: origin myth (the history of oats), cast of characters (Snap, Crackle, Pop), conflict (crispy vs. soggy), climax (the splash of milk), denouement (a spoon clinking in an empty bowl). And I realized I had been reading these novellas since before I could actually read, which meant my earliest literacy was a capitalist fan fiction about breakfast. That seemed hilarious and tragic in the same instant, a feeling I have come to recognize as the essential flavor of adulthood.

So I stood there, milkless, laughing under the drone of refrigeration, and I thought: what if every object is a story I’ve been co-authoring without noticing? The rubber boots by the door, the half-dead succulent on the windowsill, the parking ticket still wedged under the windshield wiper because I can’t decide whether to pay it or frame it as an artifact of civil disobedience—each one a participial phrase in the interminable sentence of my life. And if I never consciously punctuate, the whole thing runs on, a deranged Faulknerian monologue that will only end when my heart inserts the final period.

From that moment, I became a collector of punctuation. Not the marks themselves, but the moments that feel like punctuation: the hush between the car engine turning off and the door opening; the pause a dog takes before shaking water off its fur; the instant after the dentist says “you might feel a pinch” and before the needle breaches the gum. These are semicolons, em-dashes, parentheses made of pure time. I hoard them the way other people hoard frequent-flyer miles or ex-lovers’ hoodies. I am, you might say, a punctuational miser, and my ledger is invisible.

This obsession bled, as obsessions do, into the rest of my sensory life. I began to taste punctuation: a colon is the two dots of a raisin bagel, a period is the sudden absence of sweetness after a piece of chocolate dissolves, an ellipsis is the flat soda left in a glass once the ice has surrendered its geometry. I felt punctuation in my body: a question mark is the involuntary tilt of the head when a loud noise in the night might be a murderer or might be the dishwasher finishing its cycle; an exclamation point is the spine-straightening instant when you remember you left the stove on, followed immediately by the comma of relief when you remember you didn’t. I heard punctuation in other people’s voices, the way a simple “I’m fine” can carry an invisible asterisk that leads to a footnote scrawled on the underside of the speaker’s heart.

Eventually I started to suspect that if I gathered enough of these micro-moments, I could reconstruct the complete syntactic diagram of a human being. Not just myself, but anyone. I would become a sort of punctuation taxonomist, a Linnaeus of the unsaid. I would walk through the world with butterfly nets woven from attention, stalking the rarest of specimens: the interrobang of genuine surprise in a toddler’s face, the dieresis of recognition between strangers who realize they once shared a summer camp, the pilcrow of finality when someone closes a book they never intend to reopen.

I told a friend about this project while we were kayaking on a reservoir south of town. The water was the color of windshield washer fluid, and every few minutes a jet roared overhead, descending toward the airport, its passengers unaware that they were being punctuated into someone else’s narrative. My friend listened, paddle resting across the lip of her cockpit, then asked the reasonable question: “What will you do when you finish collecting them all?” I had no answer, only the vague sense that I would finally understand why some sentences feel finished and others feel abandoned. She dipped her blade and glided away, leaving a comma of ripples that quickly unknit themselves back to flatness, and I understood that the answer was itself a kind of colon: a signal that what follows is elaboration, example, or consequence.

Because the truth is, I am terrified of finishing the collection. What if, once I possess every variety of pause, I discover that my own life is only a fragment, a dependent clause mistakenly left to dangle? What if the final period turns out to be a bullet, a cell, a silent myocardial infarction, and I never get to revise? Virginia Woolf once said that “writing is like sex—only it’s sex with the light on and with company.” Collecting punctuation feels like sex in the dark, alone, and still somehow on camera, the worst of both worlds.

Still, I can’t stop. Last week I followed an elderly man through the produce section because I noticed he placed bananas in his cart with the care of someone setting an exclamation mark at the end of a very long sentence. I shadowed him for twenty minutes, pretending to compare the firmness of avocados, until he turned and asked if I needed help. I mumbled something about potassium and fled, heart banging against my ribs like an overused ellipsis. In the parking lot, I wrote the moment down in a notebook I now carry everywhere, a Moleskine bulging with descriptions of pauses: the comma of a dog’s ears twitching backward, the period of a laptop slamming shut, the semicolon of rain stopping just long enough for someone to decide whether to reopen their umbrella.

Sometimes I think the notebook is a confession, sometimes a suicide note stretched out episodically, sometimes a love letter to a reader I’ll never meet. Mostly it feels like a mirror facing another mirror, an infinite regression of small observations that refuse to resolve into meaning. I keep writing because the alternative is to notice nothing, and noticing nothing feels like rehearsing for death.

My father died on a day when the sky was aggressively blue, a color so saturated it seemed to be Photoshopped by some cosmic intern overcompensating for grief. I was driving to the hospital, singing along to a song whose title I still refuse to look up, as if withholding the name will retroactively unmoor the moment from its context. When I arrived, a nurse with a comma-shaped birthmark on her neck told me “he just slipped away,” and I remember thinking that “slipped” is a word that contains its own lubricant, a consonant glide that mimics the action it describes. I never got to see the body. My mother said he looked peaceful, but the word “peaceful” has always seemed to me like a euphemism for punctuation, a way of saying: the sentence is over, no more clauses will be added, you can close the book.

For months afterward, I found myself collecting only terminal punctuation: the hard stop of a garage door hitting concrete, the click of a seat belt inserting itself into the buckle, the finality of a voicemail message ending with “To delete, press seven.” I became a connoisseur of endings, a sommelier of finitude. Friends worried I was romanticizing closure, but I was doing something more desperate: trying to locate the exact contour of the hole my father had left, as if by mapping its edges I could sew a patch for it.

One night I dreamed I opened the notebook and discovered that every observation had been crossed out with a single black line. The pages were otherwise intact, but the ink had bled through, obliterating my handwriting. When I woke, I thought: this is what being awake feels like for people who believe dreams are messages. I did not believe that, but I did believe in grammar, and grammar said that a crossed-out sentence is still a sentence, just one that has been demoted from utterance to artifact. I wondered whether grief is a kind of editor, red-penciling the people who once served as our predicates.

Months passed, seasons performed their hokey melodrama of leaves and snow. I kept collecting, but now the pauses felt different—heavier, as if they contained miniature black holes that bent the trajectory of surrounding words. A comma could swallow three syllables; an ellipsis might inhale an entire anecdote. I started to sense that punctuation is not passive; it eats. The semicolon that links also disciplines; the dash that interrupts also eviscerates. I began to treat the notebook like a hazardous material, storing it in a metal box under the bed, opening it only with latexed hands and a window cracked for ventilation.

Then, on the first April after the aggressively blue day, I went to the laundromat because my washing machine had coughed itself into oblivion. I was folding someone else’s socks by mistake when a boy—maybe six—tugged my sleeve and asked if I knew how to make a paper airplane. His mother, eyes liver-spotted from exhaustion, apologized, but I waved her off. We folded a page from my grocery list, the one that noted bananas, oat milk, dish soap. The boy launched the plane, and it flew in a lazy arc, threading between the humming rows of dryers before nosing into a mound of unmatched socks. He stared at the wreckage, then up at me, and in the silence between question and disappointment, I heard it: a perfect colon, the sound of one possibility dividing into two. The first clause was the airplane; the second was everything we might do next—build another, laugh, cry, go home, stay forever. The boy’s pupils widened like ink blots, and I realized that every colon is a doorway you can only see once you’re already through it.

That night, I opened the metal box and wrote the moment down. When I finished, I noticed something odd: the ink on the previous pages had begun to fade, not disappearing but lightening, as if the paper itself were exhaling. I flipped backward through months of observations and saw that they had all become the same pale gray, the color of breath on a cold window. The notebook was erasing itself in reverse chronological order, a time-lapse film of snow returning to sky. Panic rose, then crested, then slipped back like a tide unsure whether it was coming or going. I thought: if memory is punctuation, then forgetting is the white space between words, the pause that swallows the pause.

I did not sleep. Instead, I read forward through the fading pages, trying to memorize what I had once so carefully noticed: the comma of a cat’s tail twitching at the sound of a can opener, the period of a lover setting a coffee mug on the table with finality, the em-dash of a sneeze that aborts a confession. But the more I tried to fix them in mind, the faster they dissolved, as if attention were the very solvent that erased its object. By dawn, the notebook was blank except for the last entry: the boy, the airplane, the colon. I stared at that solitary sentence until the words themselves began to feel hypothetical, like a mathematical proof that predicts a particle no one has ever observed.

I should have felt grief, but what I felt instead was relief, bright and fizzy, a kind of internal champagne. All those micro-moments had returned to wherever pauses go when no one is listening. I imagined them scattered across the world like dandelion seeds, landing on other people’s shoulders at stoplights, in conference rooms, on cancer wards, prompting someone else to notice the hush between a door slam and the apology that follows. My archive had been a temporary holding cell, and now the prisoners had been paroled into the general population of human attention.

I thought of my father, of course. If grief is an editor, then perhaps mercy is a librarian who sneaks in after hours and reshelves the books we were hoarding in our beds, convinced we were still reading them. I closed the empty notebook and set it on the windowsill, pages riffling in the breeze like a bird testing whether it has wings. Outside, the day was assembling itself from the usual components: sky, trees, the neighbor’s dog lifting its leg against the mailbox. I noticed, with the clarity that sometimes follows insomnia, that the sentence had not ended; it had merely been translated into a language that requires no ink.

Since then, I still collect, but I no longer write anything down. Instead, I speak the observations aloud, immediately, like a spell. I’ll lean toward the cashier and whisper, “The pause between your ‘have’ and ‘a nice day’ is a semicolon big enough for both of us to hide inside.” Usually they smile, sometimes they flinch, occasionally they pause—there it is again—and in that pause I can feel the observation take up residence, not in a notebook but in the world, where it belongs. I imagine that someday, when I too get edited, someone will stand in a cereal aisle and unexpectedly remember a stranger who once told them that their hesitation tasted like oats and rainy Mondays, and in that moment my sentence will find its predicate, the colon will divide, and the clause will run on, unpunctuated, forever.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
